sanskrit_devanagari:
इन्द्रियाणि पराण्याहुरिन्द्रियेभ्यः परं मनः ।
मनसस्तु परा बुद्धिर्यो बुद्धेः परतस्तु सः ॥ ४२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
indriyāṇi parāṇy āhur
indriyebhyaḥ paraṁ manaḥ |
manasas tu parā buddhir
yo buddheḥ paratas tu saḥ || 42 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.042 = MBh_06,025.042). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: The working senses are superior to dull matter; mind is higher than the senses; intelligence is still higher than the mind; and he [the soul] is even higher than the intelligence.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The senses are different outlets for the activities of lust. Lust is reserved within the body, but it is given vent through the senses. Therefore, the senses are superior to the body as a whole. These outlets are not in use when there is superior consciousness, or Kṛṣṇa consciousness.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.42 The learned ones ahuh, say; that indriyani, the five [Five sense-organs: of vision, hearning, taste, smell and touch; five motor-organs: hands, feet, speech, and for excretion and generation-these latter five are also understood in the present context.] organs-ear etc., are p
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: indriyāṇi parāṇi āhuḥ — the senses are said to be superior (to the gross body). indriyebhyaḥ paraṁ manaḥ — superior to the senses is the mind. manasaḥ tu parā buddhiḥ — but superior to the mind is the intellect. yaḥ buddheḥ paratas tu saḥ — and that which is superior to the intellect is HE — the Self (ātman / Brahman) — the irreducible substrate. The lower four are kāma's instruments and adhiṣṭhānam; the Self is the layer above kāma's reach.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.42 The senses are called the important obstacles of knowledge, because when the senses keep operating on their objects, the knowledge of the self cannot arise.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the verse establishes the layered hierarchy of the actor's constitution. Senses, mind, intellect — all are within prakṛti and are kāma's instruments. The saḥ — He — beyond buddhi is the jīva, the individual self, distinct from prakṛti. The realised actor acts from this layer; the lower three are the layers at which kāma operates.

disagreements_among_translators.word: yo buddheḥ paratas tu saḥ (the one beyond buddhi)

positions.rendering: the working senses are superior to dull matter; mind is higher than the senses; intelligence is still higher than the mind; and he [the soul] is even higher than the intelligence
the senses are superior to the body, and superior to the senses is the mind. Beyond the mind is the intellect, and even beyond the intellect is the soul

the verse establishes the hierarchy: indriyāṇi > deha; manas > indriyāṇi; buddhi > manas; the Self (ātman / Brahman) > buddhi. The Self is the irreducible substrate; the lower layers are its instruments. This is the Vedantic ontology

the hierarchy holds; the saḥ — He — beyond buddhi is the jīva (the individual self), distinct from the gunas of prakṛti and from the body-mind-intellect complex; the realised one acts from this layer, not from the lower three

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The major Advaita-Vishishtadvaita disagreement appears here: the saḥ beyond buddhi is, for Shankara, the ātman identical with Brahman; for Ramanuja, the jīva as a real distinct self. Both agree it is beyond buddhi; they disagree about its ontological status. Translators flatten both to 'the soul' which obscures the distinction. STRETCHED: the engineering analog cannot fully carry the metaphysical claim about the Self; it can carry the layered architecture (input → attention → commitment → identity) honestly.

literal_meaning: The senses are said to be superior; superior to the senses is the mind; superior to the mind is the buddhi (intellect); but THAT which is superior to buddhi is HE — the Self.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The hierarchy. Each layer is parā (superior to / subtler than) the layer below. Senses superior to gross body (the verse implicitly contrasts indriyāṇi with deha); mind (manas) superior to senses; intellect (buddhi) superior to mind; THAT (saḥ) which is superior to buddhi is the Self. The verse establishes the metaphysical layering that 3.40's diagnostic implied. Doctrinally heavy: the 'Self superior to intellect' is the Vedantic claim about the ātman as the irreducible substrate, not collapsible to any of the lower layers.
